Supervised learning with deep models has tremendous potential for applications in materials science.
Recently, graph neural networks have been used in this context, drawing direct inspiration from models for molecules.
However, materials are typically much more structured than molecules.
However, materials are typically much more structured than molecules, which is a feature that these models do not leverage.
In this work, we introduce a class of models that are equivariant with respect to crystalline symmetry groups.
We do this by deﬁning a generalization of the message passing operations that can be used with more general permutation groups.
We do this by deﬁning a generalization of the message passing operations that can alternatively be seen as deﬁning an expressive convolution operation on the crystal graph.
We do this by deﬁning a generalization of the message passing operations that can alternatively be seen as deﬁning an expressive convolution operation on the crystal graph.
Empirically, these models achieve competitive results with state-of-the-art on property prediction tasks.
Deep learning has seen remarkable applications in computational chemistry, both on the side of molecular property prediction and molecule generation.
However, less attention has been put to the problem of designing neural network architectures for the larger assemblies of atoms that constitute materials.
This is a crucial problem, as deep learning could advantageously complement computationally demanding ab initio simulation methods like DFT.
In this paper, we address the problem of designing equivariant layers and use them for supervised learning.
Equivariant layers are used for supervised learning on materials.

Crystals are described using lattices as their underlying structures.
An n-dimensional lattice can be described as the set of integral combinations of the linearly independent lattice basis vectors.
A lattice is associated with a group of translations for which the multiplication rule is addition, capturing translational symmetry.
The centering vectors are the set of lattice points contained in the supercell.
In graphene, carbon atoms are arranged in a two-dimensional crystal with a honeycomb structure.
The underlying lattice has basis vectors a1 = a, where a is a lattice constant.
The set of atoms in the unit cell is S.
Black points identify a sublattice defined by basis vectors b1 and b2.
Symmetry plays a major role in the description of crystals. This symmetry is described by a space group.
A space group element can be described as a tuple (W, t), where W is the linear part of the transformation and t a translation, which maps a crystal structure to itself.
Space groups are subgroups of the Euclidean group E(n).
An element maps a vector x given by (W, t) to Wx + t.
The point group of a space group is P.
A vector x given by a certain formula can be mapped to a point group obtained from linear part operations in a space group.
The group obtained from linear part operations in space group G generally consists of rotations.
The group obtained from linear part operations in space group G generally consists of reflections.
The translation subgroup of a space group is a normal subgroup.
The factor group G/T is isomorphic to the point group P, where G is the space group.
The crystallographic restriction theorem guarantees that only certain finite groups are valid point groups in space group G.
In 2 and 3 dimensions, only n-fold rotations are allowed in space groups.
There are 17 space group types in 2 dimensions and 230 in 3 dimensions.
The Bravais group of a lattice consists of linear isometries that map the lattice to itself for symmetry classification.
Two lattices belong to the same Bravais type if their Bravais groups are the same matrix groups.
There are 5 Bravais types in 2 dimensions and 14 in 3 dimensions as enumerated in the Appendix.
The full symmetry group G of lattice a is given by the semidirect product of its translation group T with its Bravais group P.

The group G is given by the semidirect product of its translation group with its Bravais group, which is denoted by G⇤ = T⇤ o P⇤. This relationship highlights the structure of the lattice ⇤ within the group G.
The translation subgroup of group G is represented by T⇤, showcasing the translational symmetry within the group.

Although the translation subgroup of G is T⇤, the point group of G may not necessarily be equal to P⇤. This discrepancy arises due to the different symmetries present in the underlying lattice and the unit cell.

The crystal structure associated with space group G incorporates the lattice ⇤ and the unit cell U. The unit cell U influences the symmetry of the pattern, leading to a reduction in symmetry compared to the lattice ⇤.
The symmetry group of the wallpaper, designated as P 4, reflects the rotational symmetry present in the wallpaper. This symmetry group is determined by the characteristics of the unit cell U.

The limitations of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) in handling crystalline data are explored in this section. These limitations serve as a motivation for developing alternative models that address the challenges faced by GNNs when applied to crystallographic information.

Common models for representing crystalline data involve constructing a graph from Rn, the n-dimensional Euclidean space. This graph is used to encode the spatial relationships between atoms within the unit cell for further analysis.

Equivariant message passing is a generalization of the MPNN framework that can potentially be used on any data structures for which a group can capture the symmetry in sparse interactions between the basic elements.
It is not clear how to handle structures with different groups without using a larger group like S⇤.
